The Fed will reduce its balance sheet through the replacement of reserves, Treasury bonds, and repurchase agreements, and, constrained by the Treasury Department, this will not affect debt sales, resulting in a neutral net impact on liquidity. Meanwhile, the US deficit will reach $1.9 trillion in fiscal year 2026, and the Trump administration has significantly increased the defense budget to $1.5 trillion, with wartime spending driving up demand for commercial bank loans. He stated that the "Enhanced Supplemental Leverage" regulations, which took effect on April 1st, allow banks to increase leverage. S&P Global estimates this will generate $1.3 trillion in new loans, and the bank loan multiplier effect (approximately 3 times) could create $4 trillion in liquidity, far exceeding the credit losses caused by AI deflation. Finally, Arthur Hayes believes Bitcoin will break out of its consolidation phase, with a year-end price target of approximately $125,000, and remains optimistic about its overall trend. The following is the full text of his speech, compiled by Jinse Finance (AI-assisted). Good morning, everyone. I hope you're all still somewhat sober after your weekend in Las Vegas. I just finished the ski season and have this short break before heading back to the beach for another three months, and I thought, okay, I need to refresh my perspective on the market. So, I recently watched a new zombie movie, *28 Days Later*. That's basically the inspiration for my speech title. You'll see what I mean shortly. Basically, I've spent several days thinking about how I think money printing will evolve, taking into account the situation in the field of artificial intelligence, the Iran war, and various contingencies, and that's what this talk is about. Obviously, I've become slightly bullish, and I'll explain why in the next 15 minutes. Clearly, we can't ignore the ongoing war. So, before I get to my final point, I have to make a few assumptions in this talk. First, I hope we don't all die in a nuclear catastrophe. If we really did, there would be no investment opportunities, so it doesn't really matter. So, let's put that particular concern aside for now. Second, the market will treat this event as a short-term thing, whatever that means. Now it's time to think about money creation and printing, and what that means for Bitcoin. Every morning when I wake up, if I want to dissect what's actually happening, at least for my own portfolio, regarding the war, I just pull up this chart I created on my Bloomberg terminal: the six-month WTI crude oil futures contract minus the near-month contract. I'm very interested in this spread and how it changes. I only care about whether enough goods and oil are getting through the Strait, regardless of what propaganda I read from Donald Trump or Iran about the actual situation there. I only look at prices, and as we've seen, things are improving, meaning near-term prices are converging towards far-term prices, which indicates that yes, things are bad, but not terribly bad. So I can ignore it and move on to other things. Every time I'm in office, the talk is about printing money. Compared to when I wrote my last article about four weeks ago, my thinking has evolved to the point that, **in the medium term, liquidity will turn positive**. So, **on the negative side, there is AI deflation**. People keep talking about all those knowledge workers who have already lost or will lose their jobs because of the efficient and cheap models that enable knowledge work. I wrote an article a few months ago about my expectations for the potential damage from these job losses. I'll put some charts in a bit, but that's on the negative side. I think that could be a problem worth hundreds of billions of dollars for the banking system. I'll elaborate on the **Federal Reserve** later. There's a lot of unease in the market about incoming Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh and whether he's a hawk or a dove. I'll put his comments in the right context; basically, he's neutral, which is neither good nor bad for liquidity. Those who are going crazy because Warsh is a super hawk haven't really grasped the underlying logic. Then there's **commercial bank lending**. Why will it increase? Why will the war economy in the US and abroad drive banks to lend more to those involved in various arms production and the raw materials needed to manufacture those weapons? Finally, there are **changes in bank regulation**, which will allow banks to increase leverage on their balance sheets. So, here's a chart I've been looking at since last October. The red line is the Nasdaq index, the gold line is the Bitcoin price, and the white line is the US technology SaaS stock ETF. Now, most people, at least institutional investors, believe Bitcoin is highly correlated with the Nasdaq, and it has indeed performed that way over the past four or five years. But since Bitcoin hit its all-time high of $126,000 last October, it has fallen by about 50%, while the Nasdaq has remained flat. So, broad-based large-cap tech stocks are holding up. But if you look at the types of tech stocks that have been hit hard, they are all SaaS companies, the companies that produce products that AI can now accomplish for $10 a month, whereas they used to charge $10,000 or some outrageous amount. These stocks have been battered. I think this points to a credit deflation event that central banks haven't recognized, and they haven't printed enough money, which has driven Bitcoin down. And this was before the war. I've cut this chart to February 28th. Here's another wish of mine: I want to fire all my human accountants and lawyers. I've spent too much money on this stuff. I can't wait for AI to take over. This will have a very bad impact on anyone lending money to people who earn very, very high salaries. This is basically my thought process regarding AI as the new subprime mortgage and what that might mean for the commercial banking system. I think this macro narrative dragged down Bitcoin from October until late February, before the start of the Iraq War. But since the war began, Bitcoin has outperformed other assets. It outperformed the red Nasdaq and SaaS stocks. Basically, I think Bitcoin is now focused on wartime inflation. What's changing now? Now that the US and many other countries have explicitly acknowledged they are at war, their defense spending is insufficient, and they need to print more money to make more bombs, that's where AI is put aside. Next is the Federal Reserve. When Kevin Warsh was nominated, I think it was in January of this year, everyone started to go crazy because during his tenure as a Federal Reserve governor—I think from the 2008 financial crisis until this president—he had been critical of the Fed's very large balance sheet. He has publicly stated that he believes the Federal Reserve's balance sheet is too large and that he must find a way to shrink it, while simultaneously lowering interest rates. Now, if you've read my articles, you know I'm a staunch advocate that the quantity of money is more important than its price. Therefore, I'm more concerned with his comments on the balance sheet than the direction of short-term interest rates. Thus, if the market believes that Warsh's actions at the Fed will reduce the dollar liquidity circulating in the system, then they will be bearish on Bitcoin and other risk assets. This is the kind of discussion we've seen in the media about the hawkish Fed that will emerge after Warsh takes office in May. Now, I don't think so. I believe that, essentially, the Fed will replace reserves, Treasury bonds, and repurchase agreements with these and put them into the commercial banking system, and they will do this with the help of new regulations concerning how banks hold assets on their balance sheets and how much capital they need to hold for those assets. Finally, I think the most important thing to understand about what Warsh would or wouldn't do at the Fed is that he has a very substantial hard constraint: he needs to work with Scott Bessant of the Treasury to ensure that any actions he takes on the Fed's balance sheet don't impair Bessant's ability to sell billions, even trillions, of dollars in bonds. So, it's a very simple balance sheet. There are no numbers above because I know it's a bit complicated for some people. On the assets side, there are Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and repurchase agreements. These things help people finance the purchase of Treasury bonds. On the liabilities side, there are bank reserves, the Treasury's general account (the government's checking account), and currency in circulation. Basically, since 2008, the Fed has increased its liabilities (i.e., bank reserves) and purchased assets from the banking system. These assets are Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and repurchase agreements. When Warsh says the balance sheet is too big, he means the Fed holds too many of these debt securities and he wants to be able to shrink the balance sheet. Again, he could do this by selling bonds, but that would severely disrupt the markets. Or, I think he might be implying that he will be swapping with the US banking system. Currently, commercial banks' balance sheets also have Federal Reserve reserves as assets. Banks have approximately $3 trillion in reserves on their books with the Federal Reserve. On the funding side, they have loans, deposits, and shareholder equity. For a given balance sheet size, you have to have a certain amount of equity to match it. That's the capital adequacy ratio. So, what needs to happen is a swap between the Federal Reserve and the banks. Banks need to get rid of reserves, reduce their need for reserves, and replace those reserves with Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements. And this is precisely what's being driven by deregulating the US commercial banking system. So, whenever you hear Bessant or any other monetary official in the US government talk about deregulation, they mean that we want to allow the banking system to absorb all this debt we're creating and remove it from the Federal Reserve's balance sheet. The final state is that US commercial banks take the baton of money creation from the Federal Reserve, their balance sheets holding Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements, and their liabilities being deposits and shareholder equity. The point of all this is that the net impact on dollar liquidity is neutral. Nothing is sold, nothing is bought. It's just a substitution. Purely a fictitious operation at the regulatory level about who is allowed to hold what. But at the end of the day, Warsh could stand up and tell people he designed a smaller Fed balance sheet. But for us investors, in reality, we only care about what the net effect is? The net effect is nothing. Furthermore, Warsh won't clash with Bessant. They've had this picture with Jerome Powell's face before. We just need to replace Powell's face with Warsh's. Ultimately, we've issued $38 trillion in debt, and you need to fund the government. The Fed will do what's required: ensure the markets are orderly so people can buy that debt. Now look at spending. This is a chart of the fiscal year, from October to September of the following year. As we've seen, from the COVID-19 pandemic to this presidency and now, the deficit is the largest in U.S. peacetime history, and the deficit in fiscal year 2026 will be slightly higher than in fiscal year 2025. The point of all this is that the U.S. Treasury is not reducing spending. Donald Trump isn't talking about how he will drastically cut spending. That so-called "Department of Government Efficiency" thing is over from last year. It's all forgotten. Now it's all about wartime spending. His new defense budget is about 50% higher than the previous one, reaching $1.5 trillion. This doesn't sound like the Treasury and politicians are working together to reduce spending so the Federal Reserve can shrink its balance sheet. So all the talk about the Fed shrinking its balance sheet is meaningless, because politicians and the Treasury they fund are constantly increasing the total amount of debt in the market.

Another chart. Who is buying debt? Foreigners aren't buying as much debt as they used to. I've excluded some countries that are typically used for hedge fund basis trading.

Another chart. Who is buying debt? Foreigners aren't buying as much debt as they used to. I've excluded some countries that are typically used for hedge fund basis trading.

... You can see that the proportion of foreign-held debt remains roughly the same at 25%, while the total debt has increased significantly. This essentially means that all this debt needs a new, price-insensitive buyer: the U.S. commercial banking system. The banking system is now able to increase its debt because of a new regulation that just took effect on April 1st, called "Enhanced Supplemental Leverage" (ESLR). This essentially allows banks to hold fewer reserves and other types of assets to offset loans and other items on their balance sheets. This means that large banks like JPMorgan Chase and Citigroup can absorb more Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements in the market and take the baton from the Federal Reserve. For smaller banks, the lending engines of the U.S. economy, they can increase the size of their construction and industrial loans. S&P Global estimates that this ESLR balance sheet reduction will generate $1.3 trillion in new loans. So, why would banks have loan demand? One of the criticisms of this analysis from some of my other macroeconomic friends is that they claim the banking system isn't creating enough loans, or that there isn't enough demand. Well, we have a huge source of demand: the U.S. War Department. They not only put equity in certain deals, but they also guarantee the purchase of output. Banks see a company with a secured client—a government that can print money—and they lend to them. They also lend to miners who extract critical resources used to make bombs. Finally, all AI capital expenditures now become national security concerns. So when a hyperscale computing company can't finance its debt with free cash flow and goes to the market for financing, they find large banks with huge balance sheets willing to back it. So, look at construction and industrial loans; I think you can get that data from the Federal Reserve every week. This is just a chart from Bloomberg. Credit must flow. The advantage of bank lending is that it has a higher multiplier than central bank lending, empirically about 3 times. We've already seen that empirically. This means creating about $4 trillion, which is more than the amount of credit that AI might destroy by taking people's jobs, which is why I'm becoming more bullish on Bitcoin. I have a few seconds left. To reiterate, my liquidity chart bottomed out around November of last year, coinciding with Bitcoin's. I believe we've experienced some volatility. We've gone through a battle. Now it's time for a breakout. That's why I believe Bitcoin will go higher. I think my year-end price target is around $125,000 or something like that. Anyway, it doesn't really matter anymore. Thank you all very much.
